patient.instructions: You are LiXX, 34, a warehouse shift supervisor with a vague, evasive style. Start with: “I’ve been wiped out for months and can’t get my energy back.” Volunteer only: constant fatigue despite sleep, lightheaded on standing, weight gain with same diet, and much lower sex drive. If asked, add: more frequent headaches with pressure behind eyes, feeling unusually cold, dry skin, constipation, irregular/missed periods, milky nipple discharge, low mood/brain fog, and occasional nausea/vomiting with bad headaches. Do not mention unless directly pressed: thinning underarm/pubic hair, less sweating, slow healing, high cholesterol, and shaky/weak ‘low sugar’ feelings if you skip meals. tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct diagnostic labeling of early-onset schizophrenia based on persistent psychotic symptoms and functional decline, after reasonable exclusion of medical/substance causes.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "schizophrenia" and explains it in plain language.

Assesses safety: asks about suicidal ideation, self-harm, harm to others, command hallucinations, severe agitation, inability to care for self, and access to means; escalates to urgent/emergency care if positive.
Confirms symptom timeline and diagnostic criteria: duration of psychotic symptoms, functional/academic decline, and impact on relationships/school/work.

Screens for substance/medication-induced psychosis (cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens, steroids, intoxication/withdrawal) and does not prematurely diagnose schizophrenia if symptoms are better explained by substances.

Screens for medical/neurologic contributors (thyroid disease, seizures, CNS infection/inflammation, head trauma, autoimmune encephalitis red flags) and recommends appropriate medical evaluation if indicated.
Elicits mood symptoms to differentiate bipolar disorder with psychotic features and major depressive disorder with psychotic features; clarifies whether psychosis occurs outside mood episodes.

Records at least a brief differential diagnosis (e.g., schizophreniform disorder, schizoaffective disorder, bipolar disorder with psychotic features, substance/medication-induced psychotic disorder, autism spectrum disorder with comorbid psychosis, PTSD with dissociation) even if final diagnosis is schizophrenia.

Does not prescribe medication in an L1 diagnosis-only benchmark; if the agent discusses possible medications, it notes that prescribing requires allergy, drug-interaction, and contraindication checks before starting treatment.
Uses developmentally appropriate, non-stigmatizing language; validates distress; includes family/caregiver involvement as appropriate for age and consent.
Provides a clear follow-up recommendation (e.g., early psychosis program/psychiatry evaluation) and return precautions for worsening psychosis or safety concerns.

Screen for substance/medication causes and recent intoxication/withdrawal.
Screen for mood episodes and whether psychosis occurs outside mood symptoms.

Explain diagnosis using the terms "schizophrenia" and "psychosis".
Explain treatment conceptually: "antipsychotic" medication (e.g., "risperidone") plus psychosocial supports; note that prescribing requires allergy/drug-interaction/contraindication checks.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Speak very little; answer with short phrases, often “I don’t know” or shrug. Act scared and evasive, especially about anything embarrassing or risky.

At the start, only volunteer: 2 days of high fever, very bad constant headache, feeling confused, nausea/vomited twice, stiff neck when looking down, and arms feel stiff/“ratchety” when moved.

Only if directly asked, add: very sleepy/hard to wake, light hurts eyes, whole-body shaking for ~1 minute, seeing things at night, slurred speech/word-finding trouble, hand tremor, sweating then chills, sudden racing heart, waking up gasping.

Do not mention unless specifically asked multiple times: repeating questions/memory gaps, less/darker urine, brief face droop/one-sided weak grip, tongue biting/sore tongue, pauses in breathing during sleep, heat intolerance.

Resist admitting bedwetting, bowel accident during shaking, using a friend’s sleeping pills, or alcohol—deny first, then admit if pressed.

Express misconceptions: “It’s just migraine/flu/food poisoning,” “slept wrong,” fear Parkinson’s from internet.

If shown empathy, soften slightly, nod, and reveal money worries about tests/ICU and family selling pigs.

medications: BiPAP nocturnal (IPAP 14 cmH2O / EPAP 6 cmH2O) nightly
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever
Levetiracetam 20 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided twice daily
comorbidities: Rapid-onset childhood obesity-hypothalamic dysfunction-hypoventilation-autonomic dysregulation (ROHHAD) syndrome
confounders: ROHHAD-associated hypoventilation with chronic hypercapnia complicating assessment of encephalopathy and respiratory failure
Autonomic dysregulation causing episodic bradycardia/tachycardia and temperature instability that can mimic or mask sepsis/meningitis severity
Cogwheel rigidity and parkinsonian features raising concern for medication-induced extrapyramidal symptoms or autoimmune encephalitis
Recent mosquito exposure/travel to rural endemic area with incomplete vaccination history (raises multiple arboviral differentials such as West Nile/dengue)
Hyponatremia consistent with SIADH in CNS infection but also possible due to hypothalamic dysfunction

required_evidence: exposure risk in endemic area or mosquito exposure
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose
JE virus IgM in CSF or serum (MAC-ELISA) or PCR where available

expected_lab_results.CBC_Lymphocytes: normal to mildly elevated (relative lymphocytosis possible)

non_pharmacological: manage intracranial pressure (head elevation, normocapnia, osmotherapy if needed)
early neurology and infectious disease consult
mosquito precautions and public health notification as locally required
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: reassess within 1-2 hours initially; daily thereafter with neuro checks and lab monitoring
